Author: Alessandro Giacomella
Title: Coding in the Age of AI Agents: what I learned after letting them write (some of) my code
Text:
Coding in the Age of AI Agents: what I learned after letting them write (some of) my code
TL;DR
AI agents can be unbelievably helpful… and unbelievably chaotic. The trick isn’t to use “the best tool” but to understand what each one is actually good at — and how not to let them drag your codebase into a parallel universe. Copilot is great for small stuff, Cursor is bold and fast, Claude thinks before typing, and Junie is surprisingly careful. The rest is up to your process: tight prompts, small steps, tests, and not giving the AI the keys to production.
Before we start: a small confession
I didn’t expect to enjoy coding with AI agents.
Early attempts felt like babysitting a very enthusiastic intern: lots of energy, questionable choices, and a surprising ability to break things silently.
But over the last year, working with several tools every day, I realized that the real challenge isn’t “learning how to prompt.” It’s learning how to work with these agents the same way you’d work with real teammates — understanding their personalities, their strengths, and their blind spots.
For several months, I actually “blocked” the use of AI code generation for my team. Not because I’m anti-AI (clearly), but because the early results were unpredictable enough that I didn’t want them silently slipping into production. It wasn’t a moral stance; it was a survival tactic. When half your developers are experimenting with different tools, each one with a different “personality,” the codebase starts to feel less like a project and more like a patchwork quilt.
Only after I had tested these agents myself — really tested them, across different scenarios — then I started reopening the doors. Slowly. With guardrails. And with the clear rule that AI could assist, propose, explore, but not author critical paths without a human brain signing off.
That’s what this article is about: not a tutorial, but a field report.
Practical lessons, some bruises, a few surprises.
The Problem: AI is fast, but your codebase has an history
If you’ve ever maintained a real-world project, you know this:
Not anyone writes great architecture — we inherit it, negotiate with it, and occasionally swear at it.
AI agents don’t know any of that.
Ask an agent to “clean up this module,” and it may do something technically reasonable, but totally disconnected from the unwritten rules your team follows. In one case, I asked an agent to reorganize validation logic; it reorganized half the backend. Admirable initiative, wrong planet.
That’s why, before talking about tools, we need to talk about constraints.
How I’m learning to keep AI’s energy in the right direction
1. Clarity beats cleverness
I stopped trying to “prompt cleverly.”
I just explain exactly what I want, like I would to a new colleague.
What worked:
- describing the constraints (“please stick to existing patterns”)
- pointing out landmines (“this function looks simple, but it’s used everywhere”)
- giving a rough shape of the solution
AI loves boundaries.
2. Small tasks only
Anything big becomes unpredictable.
And unpredictability in code is never fun.
So now I always split work:
One utility
One function
One refactor
One file
If I need something bigger, I break it into stages and check each one.
Boring? Yes.
Effective? Absolutely.
3. Branches are mandatory
If an AI agent ever touched my main
branch directly, I’d simply shut the laptop and walk into the sea.
Everything goes through PRs, and I read the diffs line by line. Sometimes I even read them twice — agents tend to “tidy up” small details you didn’t ask for.
4. Tests are my safety net
My test suite has become, unintentionally, the judge of all AI-generated code.
It caught subtle issues that I missed.
It also caught obvious issues that made me slightly disappointed in myself.
If you don’t have tests, your AI has no guardrails.
And neither do you.
5. Architecture changes require a human adult
If an agent starts introducing new abstractions, I call a timeout.
Even when they’re good ideas.
Architecture is the last thing you want written by an algorithm that may or may not understand the emotional load behind a 2019 commit message.
The Agents: who they are when you actually use them
This isn’t a formal comparison.
It’s closer to how you’d describe coworkers to a friend.
Junie: the calm Senior Developer who skims twice before typing
Using Junie inside a JetBrains IDE feels like pair programming with someone who has strong opinions about consistency and refuses to rush anything.
Junie rarely surprises me — and that’s high praise.
It doesn’t try to rewrite your whole project.
It listens, it follows patterns, and when it can’t, it tells you.
If your team leans toward caution and clarity, Junie fits right in.
Cursor: the ambitious teammate who moves fast (sometimes too fast)
Cursor is confident. It reads across files, notices patterns, and often suggests changes you didn’t explicitly ask for — which is sometimes great and sometimes mildly terrifying.
Ask it to “improve this,” and it may also update related functions, clean up a helper file, and propose a different error structure.
Cursor is fantastic for momentum.
But you absolutely need a strong review culture.
Claude Code: the thoughtful engineer who gives you pros and cons before writing anything
Claude does something the others rarely do:
it explains itself.
Before writing a line, it tells you:
- what it’s about to do
- why this design makes sense
- what edge cases matter
- how it’s thinking about security
It’s slow in the best way.
I use Claude when something feels off and I want a second brain that doesn’t panic and doesn’t rush.
GitHub Copilot: the reliable sprinter
Copilot is the assistant I used without thinking.
Inline suggestions, quick patterns, little helpers — it’s the productivity equivalent of drinking water.
It doesn’t aspire to restructure your project. It just helps you finish the next ten lines faster.
When I’m in the flow, Copilot keeps up.
When I need deep reasoning, I switch to Claude.
When I need cross-file edits, I jump to Cursor.
When I want safe refactoring, Junie.
Copilot is the glue, not the architect.
A Real Example: “write a function that validates User Input”
I gave all four tools the same prompt during a recent experiment:
“Write a function that validates user input, sanitizes text fields, and returns structured errors.”
Here’s what happened — in human words.
Junie
Junie wrote a tidy function matching my project’s conventions.
It didn’t invent anything.
It didn’t wander.
It added small, correct touches (like trimming strings) that felt like improvements, not surprises.
Cursor
Cursor immediately created a helper, a type definition, and a small utility for sanitization.
Useful? Yes.
Expected? No.
Safe? With review.
Cursor has ideas.
Not all of them should ship on Friday at 6pm.
Claude
Claude started by outlining potential attack vectors, explaining sanitization strategies, and why certain input patterns required special handling.
Then it wrote the function.
It felt like reading a mini RFC followed by code.
Sometimes verbose, but usually correct.
Copilot
Copilot wrote a minimal, functional version.
No frills.
Easy to read.
Good enough for 80% of use cases.
I’d still harden it before production, but as a starting point? Excellent.
A practical daily workflow
At this point, my workflow could look like this:
- Copilot for everyday coding
- Junie for safe refactoring
- Cursor for wide changes when I’m exploring
- Claude when something is confusing and I need clarity
It’s not about using them all — it’s about using them intentionally.
Where this is going
The next few years will bring agents that can follow long-term goals, track project context over weeks, and collaborate with CI pipelines. They won’t replace developers — but they will change what “coding” feels like.
Less typing.
More thinking.
More reviewing.
More orchestrating multiple agents with different strengths.
The job isn’t going away.
It’s evolving.
And honestly?
It’s getting more interesting.
Conclusion
AI agents are powerful, but they’re not magic and they’re not your lead architect. Treat them like ambitious teammates: incredibly valuable when guided, a bit dangerous when left unsupervised.
Adopt a good workflow.
Pick the right tool for the right problem.
And above all, keep ownership of your code.
You’re still the engineer (and you should be paid for that). They’re just the fast helpers.
